outline.heading: Abstract
outline.preview:
# Abstract We develop the free and open-source model Multi-tissue Splicing (MTSplice) to predict the effects of genetic variants on splicing of cassette exons in 56 human tissues. MTSplice combines MMSplice, which models constitutive regulatory sequences, with a new neural network that models tissue-specific regulatory sequences. outline.preview:

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Predicting tissue-specific effects of genetic variants on splicing is crucial for understanding tissue-specific diseases, but existing models like MMSplice only capture constitutive splicing and lack tissue-specificity. The goal is to develop a model that combines constitutive and tissue-specific splicing predictions to improve variant effect predictions across 56 human tissues.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: MTSplice combines MMSplice (constitutive) with a new neural network (tissue-specific) to predict variant effects on cassette exon splicing in 56 tissues. It outperforms MMSplice on tissue-specific variations in GTEx, especially in brain tissues, and predicts autism-associated de novo mutations are enriched for brain-specific splicing effects.
process_signals: Combining constitutive and tissue-specific models improves predictions
Largest improvements on brain tissues
Application to autism data shows brain-specific enrichment
methods: MTSplice = MMSplice + new neural network for tissue-specific sequences
metrics: Outperforms MMSplice on GTEx tissue-specific variations
Enrichment of brain-specific effects in autism mutations

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: Splicing is often tissue-specific, and defects contribute to diseases like autism. Existing tools (e.g., SPANR) lack tissue-specific predictions or are not publicly available. MMSplice predicts variant effects but is tissue-agnostic. Deep learning models for other processes use multi-task learning across tissues, motivating a similar approach for splicing.
process_signals: Problem: need for tissue-specific variant effect prediction
Constraint: MMSplice only models constitutive splicing
Constraint: SPANR not evaluated per tissue, not publicly available for tissue-specific predictions
Hypothesis: multi-task learning across tissues can capture tissue-specific regulatory sequences
failures_or_limits: MMSplice does not capture tissue-specific regulatory elements
SPANR performance on individual tissues unclear

section_gists.heading: Tissue-specific alternatively spliced exons
section_gists.gist: ASCOT dataset provides Ψ for 61,823 cassette exons across 56 tissues. 29% of exons show tissue-specific deviations >10% from average. Brain tissues cluster distinctly, and muscle/heart tissues cluster together, suggesting shared regulation that a multi-task model could exploit.
process_signals: Data source: ASCOT provides tissue-specific Ψ for training
Observation: thousands of tissue-specific events available
Observation: brain and muscle/heart tissues show co-variation, supporting multi-task learning
metrics: 17,991 exons (29%) deviate by ≥10% in at least one tissue

section_gists.heading: Differential splicing associated with genetic variants show little tissue-specific variations
section_gists.gist: Analysis of GTEx variants shows only 3.4% of exon-variant-tissue pairs have tissue-specific ΔΨ deviating >20% from average. This suggests most variants have similar effects across tissues, consistent with limited tissue-specific sQTLs. However, disease variants may still have tissue-specific effects.
process_signals: Observation: little tissue-specific variation in GTEx variant effects
Constraint: limited tissue-specific sQTLs (7-21%)
Implication: benchmarking tissue-specific models on GTEx may show only mild improvements
methods: Computed ΔΨ between homozygous alternative and reference alleles for exons with single variants
metrics: 3.4% of exon-variant-tissue pairs show tissue-specific ΔΨ deviation >20%
failures_or_limits: GTEx data from healthy donors may not capture disease-relevant tissue-specific effects

section_gists.heading: TSplice predicts tissue-specific Ψ
section_gists.gist: TSplice is a CNN with spline transformations to model positional effects. Trained on ASCOT (excluding chr2,3,5) to predict tissue-specific logit Ψ deviations. Evaluated on held-out chromosomes: median Spearman ρ=0.22 for log odds ratios, higher for CNS tissues. On natural Ψ scale, 73.9% of variable exons ranked correctly (median ρ=0.25). TSplice outperforms a ridge regression baseline using known motifs in all 56 tissues.
process_signals: Design: CNN with spline transformations for positional effects
Training: multi-task learning on 56 tissues, using logit Ψ deviations
Evaluation: better performance on CNS tissues
Comparison: TSplice outperforms linear model with known motifs
methods: TSplice: 1D conv (64 filters, length 9), spline transformations, two fully connected layers
Input: 300nt flanking + 100nt exon body, one-hot encoded
metrics: Median Spearman ρ=0.22 for log odds ratios
73.9% exons ranked correctly (median ρ=0.25) on natural Ψ
Outperforms ridge regression in all 56 tissues
failures_or_limits: Performance modest overall, better for CNS tissues

section_gists.heading: Tissue-specific variant effect prediction
section_gists.gist: MTSplice combines MMSplice (constitutive) and TSplice (tissue-specific) via a linear model on logit scale. Evaluated on GTEx variants: MTSplice improves RMSE over MMSplice for 39/51 tissues (P=1.76e-5), especially in brain tissues (10/12). Improvement is modest overall but more pronounced for variants with large tissue-specific effects.
process_signals: Integration: MTSplice = MMSplice + TSplice on logit scale
Validation: significant improvement over MMSplice in most tissues
Partial result: improvement modest, larger for tissue-specific variants

section_gists.heading: MTSplice predicts brain-specific signals for autism patients
section_gists.gist: Applied MTSplice to de novo mutations from ASD families. MMSplice shows proband variants disrupt splicing more than controls (P=0.042), stronger in LoF-intolerant genes. MTSplice reveals larger effect sizes in brain tissues, retina, and muscle. Even for variants with small MMSplice effects, MTSplice captures brain-specific signals (P=0.036 in frontal cortex).
process_signals: Validation: MTSplice identifies tissue-specific disease signals
Observation: brain-specific enrichment in ASD probands
Partial result: MTSplice captures signals missed by MMSplice
methods: Data: 3884 splice-region de novo mutations from ASD simplex families
Analysis: compare proband vs sibling predicted Δlogit(Ψ) per tissue
metrics: MTSplice: larger effect sizes in brain tissues
Frontal cortex: P=0.036 for variants with small MMSplice effects

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: MTSplice improves tissue-specific variant effect prediction, especially in brain. Limitations: TSplice trained on <10k tissue-specific exons (vs millions for MMSplice), observational data may learn correlative not causal features, limited tissue-specific MPRA data for benchmarking. Future: integrate RBP expression/CLIP-seq, generate tissue-specific MPRA data.
process_signals: Limitation: small training data for TSplice
Limitation: observational data may not capture causal variants
Limitation: lack of tissue-specific perturbation data for benchmarking
Future: transfer learning with RBP data, tissue-specific MPRA
failures_or_limits: Potential overfitting due to small data

section_gists.heading: The TSplice model
section_gists.gist: TSplice predicts tissue-specific logit Ψ deviations. Input: two branches (acceptor/donor side) with 300nt flanking + 100nt exon. Shared conv layer (64 filters, length 9), spline transformations for positional effects, concatenation, two FC layers. Output: 56-dim Δ_tissue logit(Ψ). Training adds logit(mean Ψ) to output before sigmoid to learn differential splicing.
process_signals: Design choice: shared conv layer for upstream/downstream
Design choice: spline transformations to model position-dependent motif effects
Training strategy: predict deviations from mean to focus on tissue-specific signals
methods: Architecture: 1D conv (64x9), spline transformations, 2 FC layers
Training: add logit(mean Ψ) to output, then sigmoid

section_gists.heading: Tissue-specific variant effect prediction
section_gists.gist: MTSplice models logit(Ψ_alt) as sum of logit(average Ψ_ref), MMSplice(ref,alt), and TSplice(alt,tissue). ΔΨ predicted by subtracting measured Ψ_ref. MMSplice-only prediction omits TSplice term.
process_signals: Integration: additive model on logit scale
Assumption: variant effect decomposes into constitutive and tissue-specific components

section_gists.heading: Availability of data and materials
section_gists.gist: MTSplice integrated with MMSplice, available on GitHub and Kipoi. ASCOT data from http://ascot.cs.jhu.edu/.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The paper hypothesizes that combining a constitutive splicing model (MMSplice) with a tissue-specific model (TSplice) will improve prediction of tissue-specific variant effects.
Multi-task learning across tissues can capture shared tissue-specific regulatory sequences, especially for related tissues like brain regions.
Tissue-specific variant effects are rare in healthy GTEx data but may be enriched in disease variants, making MTSplice useful for prioritizing pathogenic variants.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Why does MTSplice perform worse than MMSplice in testis?
How much does the limited training data (observational, <10k exons) limit performance, and can transfer learning with RBP data improve it?
Are the mild improvements in GTEx due to the rarity of tissue-specific variant effects in healthy individuals, and would tissue-specific MPRA data show larger gains?
Can the model generalize to other splicing types beyond cassette exons?

events.summary: TSplice was trained on the ASCOT dataset using all chromosomes except for chromosome 2, 3, and 5.
TSplice predictions positively correlated with measurements in all tissues, with median Spearman correlation of 0.22, and higher performance for CNS tissues.
TSplice outperforms a ridge regression baseline using known motifs in all 56 tissues.
Visualization of TSplice gradients showed activation at binding site motifs of PTBP1/2, NOVA1/2, and MBNL1.
In silico mutations with NOVA1/2 and MBNL1 motifs showed TSplice predictions consistent with known repressive roles in brain and muscle.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Predicting tissue-specific effects of genetic variants on splicing is crucial for understanding tissue-specific diseases, but existing models like MMSplice only capture constitutive splicing and lack tissue-specificity.

canonical_events.summary: Existing tools lack tissue-specific splicing predictions; MMSplice is tissue-agnostic.
Multi-task learning across tissues can capture shared tissue-specific regulatory sequences.
29% of exons show tissue-specific deviations >10% from average; brain and muscle/heart tissues cluster.
Limited tissue-specific sQTLs (7-21%) and GTEx from healthy donors may not capture disease-relevant tissue-specific effects.
TSplice model architecture: two input branches with shared convolution, spline transformations, and multi-task output.
TSplice trained on ASCOT (excluding chr2,3,5) with KL divergence loss, Adam optimizer, and ensemble of 8 models.
TSplice predictions positively correlated with measurements in all tissues (median Spearman ρ=0.22), higher for CNS tissues.
TSplice outperforms ridge regression baseline using known motifs in all 56 tissues.
TSplice captured known tissue-specific regulatory motifs (PTBP1/2, NOVA1/2, MBNL1) via gradient visualization and in silico mutations.
MTSplice combines MMSplice (constitutive) and TSplice (tissue-specific) via additive model on logit scale.
MTSplice outperforms MMSplice in 39/51 tissues (P=1.76e-5), especially brain (10/12 tissues).
Improvement of MTSplice over MMSplice is significant but modest in RMSE (0.138 vs 0.140).
MTSplice identifies brain-specific splicing disruption in ASD probands vs siblings (P=0.042), stronger in LoF-intolerant genes.
MTSplice captures brain-specific disease signals missed by MMSplice (P=0.036 in frontal cortex).
Lack of tissue-specific perturbation data limits benchmarking; TSplice trained on <10k exons may overfit; testis performance worse than MMSplice.

findings.problem: Step 10 incorrectly attributes the P=0.042 result to MTSplice, but the evidence (EV_C003_E06_01) clearly states it was MMSplice that predicted stronger disruption in probands. MTSplice's role was in tissue-specific analysis (K14). This misrepresents the model's contribution.
findings.revision_instruction: Correct step 10 to state that MMSplice (not MTSplice) predicted stronger splicing disruption in probands (P=0.042). Clarify that MTSplice was used for tissue-specific analysis in step 11.

findings.problem: Step 9 reports RMSE values 0.138 vs 0.140, but the evidence (EV_AUTO_015) quotes '141 versus 0.139'. The numbers are inconsistent with the evidence bank.
findings.revision_instruction: Update RMSE values in step 9 to match the evidence: 0.141 vs 0.139, or clarify the source of the 0.138/0.140 values if they come from a different evaluation.

findings.problem: Step 2 mentions '29% of exons show tissue-specific deviations >10%' but the evidence provided does not include these exact numbers. The evidence only describes clustering patterns, not the 29% or 10% thresholds.
findings.revision_instruction: Add evidence for the 29% and 10% deviation numbers, or remove them if unsupported. The canonical event K03 requires these numbers, so ensure they are backed by evidence.

findings.problem: Step 3 mentions '20 models trained, 8 selected' but the evidence for ensemble size (EV_C004_E05_03) is not included in the step's evidence list. The step only includes evidence up to EV_C004_E05_02.
findings.revision_instruction: Add EV_C004_E05_03 to step 3's evidence to support the ensemble size of 8 models.

findings.problem: Step 10 mentions P=0.0035 for LoF-intolerant genes, but the evidence (EV_AUTO_034) only states 'The effect size is larger for variants in LoF-intolerant genes (n = 1081).' without providing the P-value. The P-value is unsupported.
findings.revision_instruction: Either provide evidence for the P=0.0035 value or remove it from the step.

self_critic.summary: The candidate has several issues: a high-severity misattribution of results (MMSplice vs MTSplice), inconsistent RMSE values, missing evidence for key numbers (29%, 10%, P=0.0035), and incomplete evidence for ensemble size. These must be corrected to ensure accuracy and consistency with the evidence bank.

source.title: MTSplice predicts effects of genetic variants on tissue-specific splicing

initial_request.research_problem: Predicting tissue-specific effects of genetic variants on splicing is crucial for understanding tissue-specific diseases, but existing models like MMSplice only capture constitutive splicing and lack tissue-specificity.
initial_request.known_context: MMSplice is a tissue-agnostic model for variant effect prediction on splicing. SPANR does not allow tissue-specific predictions. Multi-task learning across tissues can capture shared regulatory sequences.
constraints: GTEx from healthy donors may not capture disease-relevant tissue-specific effects
input_data: ASCOT dataset of tissue-specific percent spliced-in (Ψ) values
quantifiable_goals: Improve splicing variant effect prediction in a tissue-specific manner

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Existing tools like MMSplice and SPANR lack tissue-specific splicing predictions.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No model predicts tissue-specific variant effects on splicing.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Multi-task learning across tissues can capture shared tissue-specific regulatory sequences.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a multi-task neural network (TSplice) to predict tissue-specific splicing.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Multi-task learning has been successful in other splicing models.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to analyze tissue-specific splicing data to inform model design.

evidence.quote_or_span: MMSplice has been trained in a tissue-agnostic fashion and therefore does not capture effects of variants affecting tissue-specific regulatory elements.
the publicly available SPANR does not allow performing tissue-specific predictions.

A common denominator of these models is that they are trained by multi-task learning, i.e., the models make joint predictions for all tissues or cell types using a common set of underlying predictive features. This strategy allows models to efficiently pool information about regulatory elements that are shared across cell types or tissues.

evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Data supports need for tissue-specific model; also reveals limitation of GTEx data.

skeletal muscle and the two heart tissues (left ventrial and atrial appendage) also clustered together with shared splicing patterns.

evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge limitations of the approach.

evidence.quote_or_span: when considering the tissue-specific effect predicted by MTSplice, the de novo mutations from the proband group were predicted to disrupt more severely splicing in the frontal cortex (Additional file 1: Fig. S7A, P = 0.036, one-sided Wilcoxon test)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: MTSplice demonstrates tissue-specific predictions and disease relevance.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Limitations exist in training data and benchmarking.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Lack of tissue-specific perturbation data limits benchmarking; TSplice may overfit due to small training set.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Discuss limitations: small training data, lack of tissue-specific perturbation data, testis performance worse than MMSplice.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Need large-scale tissue-specific perturbation data for better benchmarks; TSplice trained on <10k exons may overfit.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude with overall assessment.

evidence.quote_or_span: The generation of large-scale tissue or cell-type-specific perturbation data could therefore be instrumental for probing tissue-specific regulatory elements and could yield more sensitive benchmarks of predictive models.

validation_methods: Spearman correlation with ASCOT measurements
Comparison to ridge regression baseline
Gradient visualization and in silico mutagenesis for motif discovery
Benchmarking against MMSplice on GTEx variant effects
Application to ASD de novo mutations
success_verification.final_conclusion: MTSplice improves tissue-specific splicing variant effect prediction over MMSplice, particularly in brain tissues, and identifies disease-relevant splicing disruptions in ASD.
limitations: Modest RMSE improvement over MMSplice
Limited tissue-specific sQTLs (7-21%)
GTEx from healthy donors may not capture disease-relevant effects
Lack of tissue-specific perturbation data for benchmarking
TSplice trained on <10k exons may overfit
Testis performance worse than MMSplice
